meta.title: Early Math Foundations, Playful, Concrete Math for Ages 4-7 (K-2)
meta.description: Live online early math course for ages 4-7: counting with meaning, number bonds, shapes, measurement and playful problem solving, taught concrete-first in short, joyful, interactive classes. 8 classes a month, free demo first.
meta.level: Ages 4-7 (kindergarten to grade 2), all starting points
meta.commitment: 2 live one-hour classes per week (structured as playful segments) + everyday math play at home
meta.certification: Course completion certificate + stage mastery checks

program_overview.title: Early Math Foundations
program_overview.tagline: The years that decide whether math becomes a friend. We make it one.
program_overview.description: A 12-month live program for children aged 4-7, the years when a child's whole relationship with mathematics is quietly decided. Early math done right is not worksheets; it is counting that means something, quantities seen and handled, patterns spotted, shapes built and stories solved. Our mentors teach concrete-first in genuinely interactive classes structured as short playful segments that respect young attention spans: real objects, ten-frames, games and math talk, with the child doing and explaining rather than watching. Children leave this course fluent in early number, confident in their own thinking, and, the part parents notice most, actually liking math.
what_makes_this_different: Concrete before abstract, always: quantities are handled, drawn, and only then written as symbols
Classes built for young attention: an hour of varied segments, games, movement, building, story problems, never an hour of sitting still
Counting taught with meaning: one-to-one correspondence, cardinality and the ideas most programs skip straight past
Number bonds and part-whole thinking started early, gently, the foundation everything to age 18 stands on
Math talk from day one: even five-year-olds explain their thinking here, in their own words
One dedicated mentor who knows the child, their pace and their spark
Parents equipped too: simple everyday math-play prompts, because kitchens teach more than worksheets
The honest opposite of drill academies, no pressure, no rankings, no tears

learning_path.phase_1: Counting with meaning, comparing quantities, and the first part-whole ideas, through objects and games
learning_path.phase_2: Addition and subtraction as stories, bonds to 10, place value beginnings and the teen numbers demystified
learning_path.phase_3: Numbers to 100 and beyond, early measurement, shapes and data, plus problem-solving courage and grade-2 readiness

career_outcomes: A child who says 'I like math', the outcome that predicts the next decade
Number sense foundations that make grades 3-5 feel natural
Fine reasoning habits: explaining, checking, trying another way
A smooth on-ramp into Mental Maths Mastery or the Elementary Masterclass

phase_1_foundation.title: PHASE 1: Counting That Means Something (Months 1-4)
phase_1_foundation.description: Counting looks simple and hides deep ideas: one number per object, the last number telling the total, quantities compared and conserved. This phase builds those ideas properly through objects, movement and games, the difference between reciting numbers and understanding them.

week_1.title: A Playful First Look
week_1.description: Playful diagnostic disguised as games: counting, comparing and pattern instincts observed
topics: Counting objects to see how far counting flows
Comparing two small groups for more or fewer
Copying and continuing a simple pattern
Noticing how the child talks about numbers
Spotting counting habits gently, without correcting
Finding a warm, happy starting point
week_1.practice: Line up a few toys and ask your child to count them and tell you which pile has more, keeping it a game and not a test.

week_2.title: One Number for Each Thing
week_2.description: One-to-one counting with real objects; the cardinality idea, the last number IS the amount
topics: Touching each object once while saying one number
Matching one number word to one thing
Understanding the last number tells the total
Counting a group and answering how many
Recounting a moved group to see the total stays the same
Slowing down to count carefully
week_2.practice: Count the stairs together as you climb, touching each step and cheering the final number as the total.

week_3.title: Seeing Small Numbers Instantly
week_3.description: Subitizing: seeing 3, 4 and 5 without counting, dice, dots and finger patterns
topics: Recognising 1, 2 and 3 without counting
Seeing 4 and 5 as quick patterns
Reading dice dots at a glance
Showing numbers fast with fingers
Naming flashed dot cards instantly
Talking about how the amount was seen
week_3.practice: Flash some fingers for a second, hide them, and ask how many your child saw, taking turns being the flasher.

week_4.title: More, Fewer, or the Same?
week_4.description: Comparing quantities: more, fewer, same, justified with matching, not guessing
topics: Comparing two groups for more or fewer
Matching items one to one to compare fairly
Recognising when two groups are the same
Using the words more, fewer and equal
Explaining how you know which has more
Making two groups equal by adding or taking away
week_4.practice: Put out two rows of buttons and ask your child to line them up in pairs to prove which row has more, or whether they match.

week_5_6.title: Number Symbols and Counting Both Ways
week_5_6.description: Numerals connected to quantities; counting forwards and backwards as a game
topics: Matching the written numeral to its quantity
Recognising the numerals 0 to 10
Counting forwards to ten smoothly
Counting backwards from ten like a rocket
Placing a numeral card beside the right group
Writing or tracing numerals playfully
week_5_6.practice: Do a rocket countdown from 10 to 0 before a jump or a tickle, then count back up to ten together.

week_7_8.title: The Ten-Frame as Home Base
week_7_8.description: Ten-frames as home base: five-and-some structure, seeing numbers inside numbers
topics: Filling a ten-frame from left to right
Seeing a full row of five
Reading 7 as five and two more
Spotting how many empty spaces are left
Seeing numbers hidden inside bigger numbers
Knowing how far a number is from ten
week_7_8.practice: Draw a two-by-five grid and let your child place counters to show a number like 8, then say it as five and three more.

week_9_10.title: Breaking Numbers into Parts
week_9_10.description: Part-whole play: 5 is 2-and-3, 4-and-1, decomposition as a toy, years before it is a technique
topics: Splitting a small group into two parts
Seeing 5 as 2 and 3, or 4 and 1
Finding different ways to make the same number
Putting the two parts back into the whole
Using two hands to hold the two parts
Talking about the parts out loud
week_9_10.practice: Hide 5 counters behind your back in two hands, show one hand, and let your child work out how many are hiding in the other.

week_11_12.title: First, Second, Third and Where Things Sit
week_11_12.description: Ordinal numbers, position words and the number line met as a walking game
topics: Using first, second, third and last
Describing position with in front, behind and between
Walking along a number line as a path
Finding which number comes before or after
Ordering a small line of toys
Naming who is in each place in a line
week_11_12.practice: Line up soft toys and ask playful questions like which one is third, or who is between the first and the fourth.

month_4.title: Month 4: Patterns and Sorting + Stage Checkpoint

week_13.title: Patterns That Repeat
week_13.description: Repeating patterns: copy, extend, create, the beginnings of algebraic thinking
topics: Copying a simple repeating pattern
Continuing a pattern by what comes next
Creating a brand new pattern of your own
Naming the repeating unit, like red blue red blue
Spotting patterns in colours, shapes and sounds
Fixing a pattern with a deliberate mistake in it
week_13.practice: Start a pattern with household items, like spoon fork spoon fork, and ask what comes next, then let your child build one for you.

week_14.title: Sorting by How Things Are Alike
week_14.description: Sorting and classifying by attributes; explaining the rule you sorted by
topics: Sorting objects into groups by colour, size or shape
Choosing your own rule for sorting
Explaining the rule you used
Noticing an object that does not belong
Sorting the same set a second, different way
Describing groups with words like all and none
week_14.practice: Tip out a box of toys or socks and ask your child to sort them, then guess the rule they chose before they tell you.

week_15.title: Patterns That Grow
topics: Seeing a pattern that gets bigger each step
Working out what comes next in a growing pattern
Building steps like 1, then 2, then 3 blocks
Describing how the pattern changes
Predicting a later step in the pattern
Comparing repeating and growing patterns
week_15.practice: Build a staircase of blocks that grows by one each time and ask your child how many blocks the next step needs.

week_16.title: Phase One Check Through Play
week_16.description: Stage mastery check through play: show it, say it, teach it to your bear
topics: Counting a group and telling the total
Comparing quantities with more and fewer
Showing a number on a ten-frame
Copying and extending a pattern
Explaining thinking out loud
Teaching a skill to a toy or a parent
week_16.practice: Let your child teach a teddy how to count a small group and spot which pile has more, while you happily listen in.
week_16.assessment: A gentle play-based check of counting, comparing, ten-frames and patterns, where the child shows a skill, says how it works, and teaches it to a toy, with no pressure.

phase_2_operations.title: PHASE 2: Stories, Bonds and the Teen Numbers (Months 5-8)
phase_2_operations.description: Addition and subtraction arrive as stories acted out with objects, joining, separating, comparing, long before symbols carry them. Bonds to 10 grow toward automaticity through games, and the famously confusing teen numbers get demystified with place-value materials.

month_5.title: Month 5: Addition and Subtraction as Stories

week_17_18.title: Adding and Taking Away as Stories
week_17_18.description: Joining and separating stories acted with objects; the plus and minus symbols as story shorthand
topics: Acting out a joining story with objects
Acting out a taking-away story
Meeting the plus sign as and more
Meeting the minus sign as take away
Reading a simple number story as a picture
Telling the story back in your own words
week_17_18.practice: Tell a tiny story with toys, like three ducks and two more join them, and let your child act it out and say how many altogether.

week_19_20.title: Counting On and Counting Back
week_19_20.description: Counting on (not starting over) and counting back; the empty-hand subtraction game
topics: Starting from the bigger number and counting on
Not going back to one every time
Counting back to take away
Using fingers to count on a few more
Playing the hidden-in-the-hand take-away game
Choosing to count on or count back
week_19_20.practice: Hold some counters in a closed hand, say the number inside, add a couple on top, and let your child count on from the hidden number.

month_6.title: Month 6: Bonds of 10 and Fact Families

week_21_22.title: The Pairs That Make Ten
week_21_22.description: Bonds of 10 through every game we know, the single most valuable fact set in mathematics
topics: Finding two parts that make ten
Learning pairs like 6 and 4, 7 and 3
Filling a ten-frame to see the missing part
Answering the partner of a number quickly
Playing many games to make the pairs stick
Feeling how ten is a friendly whole
week_21_22.practice: Play friends of ten: hold up some fingers on one hand and ask how many more make ten, then swap hands.

week_23_24.title: One Number Trio, Four Facts
week_23_24.description: Fact families: 3+7, 7+3, 10-7, 10-3 as one idea seen four ways
topics: Seeing three numbers that belong together
Making two addition facts from the trio
Making two subtraction facts from the trio
Understanding adding and taking away as linked
Using one known fact to find the others
Building the whole fact family from a picture
week_23_24.practice: Give your child three numbers like 2, 8 and 10 and see how many facts they can say using all three.

month_7.title: Month 7: Teen Numbers and Place Value Beginnings

week_25_26.title: Teen Numbers Are Ten and Some More
week_25_26.description: Teens demystified: 14 as ten-and-four, built with materials, where place value actually starts
topics: Building a teen number as one ten and some ones
Seeing 14 as ten and four
Grouping ten small items into one bundle
Reading teen numbers correctly
Understanding where place value begins
Matching the numeral to the ten-and-ones picture
week_25_26.practice: Bundle ten straws or sticks with a band, add a few loose ones, and ask your child to name the teen number you made.

week_27_28.title: All the Way to Twenty
week_27_28.description: Numbers to 20: counting, comparing, and bridging-through-10 previewed as a game
topics: Counting forwards and backwards to 20
Comparing two numbers up to 20
Ordering numbers up to 20
Filling a second ten-frame past ten
Previewing the make-ten bridge as a game
Spotting the ten inside a teen number
week_27_28.practice: Count a bigger pile of objects, like 16 pasta pieces, into two ten-frames, then ask which of two numbers up to 20 is bigger.

month_8.title: Month 8: Story Problems and the Explain Habit

week_29_30.title: Act It, Draw It, Write It
week_29_30.description: Word problems as theatre: act it, draw it, then write it, the three-step that prevents future word-problem fear
topics: Acting out a word problem with objects
Drawing a simple picture of the story
Writing the matching number sentence
Finding the question hidden in the story
Deciding whether to join or take away
Checking the answer against the story
week_29_30.practice: Make up a little story, such as five apples and two eaten, and guide your child to act it, draw it, then write the number sentence.

week_31_32.title: Missing Numbers and Phase Two Check
week_31_32.description: Missing-number puzzles and both-ways thinking; stage mastery check through play
topics: Finding a missing number in a simple sentence
Thinking backwards from an answer
Using fact families to fill the gap
Reviewing story problems and bonds of ten
Explaining how a puzzle was solved
Showing teen numbers as ten and ones
week_31_32.practice: Pose a gentle puzzle like something plus 3 makes 7 using counters, and let your child find the missing amount.
week_31_32.assessment: A relaxed play-based check of story problems, bonds of ten, fact families and teen numbers, where the child solves a puzzle and explains the thinking, with no pressure.

phase_3_expansion.title: PHASE 3: To 100 and Into the World (Months 9-12)
phase_3_expansion.description: The number world expands to 100 with place-value understanding, and math steps into the child's world: measuring, money, time, shapes and simple data. The course closes with problem-solving courage and a confident handoff to grade-level mathematics.

week_33_34.title: Building Numbers from Tens and Ones
week_33_34.description: Tens and ones with materials: building two-digit numbers, comparing and ordering them
topics: Building a two-digit number from tens and ones
Bundling tens and counting the leftover ones
Reading a number like 34 as three tens and four
Comparing two two-digit numbers
Ordering several numbers from small to big
Matching the numeral to the tens-and-ones model
week_33_34.practice: Use grouped straws or blocks to build a number like 27, then build another and ask your child which is bigger and why.

week_35_36.title: Skip Counting and the Hundred Square
week_35_36.description: Skip counting by 2s, 5s and 10s as rhythm and pattern; the 100-square explored like a map
topics: Skip counting by 10s to one hundred
Skip counting by 5s and by 2s
Hearing skip counting as a rhythm
Finding patterns on the hundred square
Moving up, down and across the hundred square
Spotting the tens column and its counting patterns
week_35_36.practice: Chant counting by tens up to a hundred on a walk, then try by fives, clapping to keep the rhythm.

week_37_38.title: Adding Two-Digit Numbers
week_37_38.description: Adding tens, adding ones: two-digit addition with materials first, mental jumps second
topics: Adding two numbers using tens and ones blocks
Combining the tens, then the ones
Moving from blocks to picturing the jump
Adding a whole ten to a number
Solving sums like 23 plus 14
Checking the answer with the materials
week_37_38.practice: Set out blocks for a sum like 22 plus 13 and let your child join the tens and the ones, then say the total.

week_39_40.title: Ten More and Ten Less
week_39_40.description: Ten-more ten-less fluency; friendly-number thinking previewed gently
topics: Finding ten more than a number quickly
Finding ten less than a number
Using the hundred square to jump by ten
Noticing only the tens digit changes
Meeting friendly round numbers
Jumping to the next friendly ten
week_39_40.practice: Say a number like 34 and ask for ten more, then ten less, using the hundred square to check each jump.

week_41_42.title: Measuring and Guessing
week_41_42.description: Length, weight and capacity by comparing and then by counting units; estimation as a guessing game with feedback
topics: Comparing lengths as longer and shorter
Comparing weights as heavier and lighter
Comparing capacity as holds more or less
Measuring with repeated units like paper clips
Guessing a measurement, then checking it
Using words like longest, heaviest and full
week_41_42.practice: Guess how many hand-spans long the table is, then measure together to see how close the guess was.

week_43_44.title: Money and Telling the Time
week_43_44.description: Coins and simple shopping play; o'clock and half-past on real clocks
topics: Recognising a few common coins
Paying for small pretend items
Counting a small amount of money
Reading o'clock on a real clock
Reading half-past on a real clock
Linking times to parts of the day
week_43_44.practice: Play shop with real coins for a few priced items, then check what o'clock it is on a real clock together.

week_45_48.title: Shapes and Fair Shares
week_45_48.description: 2D and 3D shapes built, named and hunted at home; halves and quarters as fair sharing, fractions met kindly
topics: Naming flat shapes like circle, square and triangle
Naming solid shapes like cube, sphere and cone
Building and hunting for shapes at home
Describing shapes by their sides and corners
Sharing something fairly into halves
Sharing into quarters as four equal parts
week_45_48.practice: Go on a shape hunt around the house, then share a snack or a drawing fairly into halves and quarters.

week_49_52.title: Graphs, Graduation and the Road Ahead
week_49_52.description: Simple pictographs from the child's own surveys; graduation project, the child teaches their favourite math game, and the road-ahead plan into grade-level math
topics: Asking a simple survey question
Collecting answers from family or class
Drawing a picture graph of the results
Reading which answer had the most
Teaching a favourite math game to others
Looking ahead to grade-level math
week_49_52.practice: Help your child survey the family on a fun question, like favourite fruit, and draw a little picture graph of the answers.
week_49_52.assessment: A gentle final celebration where the child makes a simple pictograph, teaches a favourite math game, and revisits the year's ideas, with a friendly road-ahead plan instead of a test.

faqs.question: Can a 4-year-old really learn in online classes?
faqs.answer: Yes, with the right design, which is the whole craft here. Classes run as short, varied playful segments: a counting game, a building challenge, a story problem, a movement break with skip counting. The mentor works with real objects the child holds up to the camera, and parents of our youngest students sit nearby for the first weeks. The free demo shows you exactly how it works with your child.

faqs.question: Isn't this age too early for structured math?
faqs.answer: It is exactly the right age for the right kind: playful, concrete, talk-rich mathematics. Research on early number sense is unambiguous, the part-whole and counting ideas built at 4-7 predict mathematics success for years. What would be wrong at this age is worksheets and drilling, which is why we do neither.

faqs.question: How is this different from apps like Khan Kids or busy-work workbooks?
faqs.answer: Apps entertain and practice; they cannot listen. The engine of early math learning is math talk, a child explaining 'I saw 5 because 4-and-1' to a person who responds. Our mentors have that conversation twice a week, notice exactly which ideas have landed, and adjust in real time. No app does that.

faqs.question: My child already loves numbers. Will this stretch them?
faqs.answer: Happily, the mentor teaches to stage, not age. A number-loving five-year-old might race into part-whole play and early place value while a classmate consolidates counting. Small groups and 1-on-1 make that flexibility real.

faqs.question: What do parents need to do at home?
faqs.answer: Minutes, not hours: we send simple everyday prompts, count the stairs, share the strawberries fairly, spot the pattern on the tiles. Kitchens and car rides teach more early math than worksheets ever did, and we show you how to use them.

faqs.question: What comes after this course?
faqs.answer: Graduates flow into Mental Maths Mastery (for fluency depth) or the Elementary Mathematics Masterclass (the full grades 1-5 program) with the same mentor where possible, arriving with the foundation those courses quietly assume.

faqs.question: What does the course cost?
faqs.answer: Small-group classes are ₹1,499 per month and private 1-on-1 mentorship is ₹4,999 per month in India; international students pay USD $100 per month for group and $150 for 1-on-1. Both include 8 live classes a month, recordings, and a free demo class before any payment.

faqs.question: Can we try it before paying?
faqs.answer: Yes. Every child starts with a free live demo class that doubles as the playful diagnostic, no card details, no obligation, and you will know within the hour whether your child lights up.

testimonials.name: Sonam Oswal, mother of Dhairya (verified Google review)
testimonials.text: My child Dhairya is really enjoying the classes. This is his first online class, and he eagerly looks forward to it. I can see his improvement.

testimonials.name: Shewta Singh, mother of Ishan (verified Google review)
testimonials.text: My son struggled with math for years. Integrating it into coding projects has transformed his understanding and confidence. Highly recommended!

projects_throughout_course: The child's own counting museum: collections photographed and counted
Pattern gallery: repeating and growing patterns the child created
Shape hunt portfolio from around the home
The child's first survey and pictograph
Graduation game: the child teaches their favourite math game
additional_learning_resources.total_projects_built: A year of playful artifacts the child is proud of
skills_mastered: Counting with one-to-one correspondence and cardinality
Addition and subtraction as stories, then facts
Early measurement, money, time and shapes

weekly_structure.live_classes: 2 one-hour live classes in short playful segments
weekly_structure.home_play: Everyday math prompts for parents, minutes, not worksheets
weekly_structure.materials: Household objects as manipulatives; printable ten-frames provided

support_provided.live_sessions: 8 live classes every month, 2 per week
support_provided.mentorship: One dedicated mentor who knows the child's stage and spark
support_provided.progress_tracking: Stage mastery notes shared with parents
support_provided.flexibility: Slots move around naps, school and family life

certification.phase_certificates: Stage mastery check at the end of each phase
certification.final_certificate: Course completion certificate from Modern Age Coders

prerequisites.math_background: None, this course IS the foundation
prerequisites.equipment: Computer or tablet with stable internet; everyday objects (blocks, buttons, spoons) as materials
prerequisites.time_commitment: 2 live hours plus everyday math play
prerequisites.parent_role: Nearby for the youngest children in early weeks; cheering thereafter

who_is_this_for.first_steps: Ages 4-5 taking their very first structured math steps, playfully
who_is_this_for.k_2_students: Kindergarten to grade 2 children whose school math needs a stronger foundation underneath
who_is_this_for.eager_minds: Young children already fascinated by numbers who deserve real food for it
who_is_this_for.worried_starts: Children whose first school math experiences created tears, reversible at this age, quickly
who_is_this_for.planning_parents: Families who want the foundation laid right the first time, before gaps exist

career_paths_after_completion: Ready for Mental Maths Mastery with the bonds already forming
Ready for the Elementary Mathematics Masterclass, arriving confident
A child who enters grade 3 liking math, the outcome that compounds for a decade
Early access to the maths-and-coding world for the builder types

course_guarantees.live_classes: Live, interactive classes with a real instructor, never pre-recorded videos.
course_guarantees.small_batches: Small batches only: group classes are capped at 10 students, with mini-batch (3 to 4 students) and personal 1-on-1 options.
course_guarantees.structured_curriculum: A structured, well-paced curriculum taught step by step, with hands-on practice in every session.
course_guarantees.doubt_support: Doubt support between classes over WhatsApp, so you are never left stuck.
course_guarantees.certificate: A course-completion certificate you can share.
course_guarantees.free_demo: A free demo class before you enrol, so you can decide with no pressure.
